title: Suárez handball on the goal line — WC 2010 quarter-final
call: Red card for Suárez. Penalty kick awarded to Ghana.
what_happened: With the score at 1–1 in the final seconds of extra time, Ghana's Dominic Adiyah headed the ball toward an unguarded goal. Uruguay's Luis Suárez, positioned on or near the goal line, stopped the ball with both hands. The referee awarded a penalty kick to Ghana and showed Suárez a red card. Asamoah Gyan struck the crossbar with the penalty. Uruguay won the subsequent penalty shoot-out 4–2.
explanation:
The referee's decision to show Luis Suárez a red card and award Ghana a penalty kick was fully justified under the Laws of the Game.

Firstly, Suárez's act of stopping the ball with his hands clearly constituted a deliberate handball offence. Law 12 states: "It is an offence if a player...deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm." By positioning himself on the goal line and using his hands to block Adiyah's shot, Suárez deliberately handled the ball, violating this clause. 

Moreover, this handball denied Ghana an obvious goal-scoring opportunity. Law 12 further explains: "Where a player denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence, the player is sent off wherever the offence occurs." Since Suárez's handball prevented a clear chance to score, he had to be shown a red card per this provision.

The red card was also warranted because denying an obvious goal-scoring opportunity through deliberate handball is listed among the sending-off offences in Law 12: "Denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity...by committing a deliberate handball offence...the player is sent off."

Finally, the penalty kick was correctly awarded. Law 12 specifies: "Where a player commits an offence against an opponent within their own penalty area which denies an opponent an obvious goal-scoring opportunity and the referee awards a penalty kick..." Since Suárez's handball occurred within Uruguay's penalty area and prevented Ghana's goal, a penalty kick was the proper sanction.

In summary, Suárez's deliberate handball that denied an obvious goal led to him being rightfully sent off with a red card, while Ghana was properly awarded a penalty kick based on the specific clauses in Laws 12 regarding handballs, denying goal opportunities, and sending-off offenses. The referee applied the laws precisely as written.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | It is an offence if a player: [page 116, authoritative law text] ---
- deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, for example moving the hand/arm towards the ball
- touches the ball with their hand/arm when it has made their body unnaturally bigger. A player is considered to have made their body unnaturally bigger when the position of their hand/arm is not a consequence of, or justifiable by, the player's body movement for that specific situation. By having their hand/arm in such a position, the player takes a risk of their hand/arm being hit by the ball and being penalised
- scores in the opponents' goal:
- directly from their hand/arm, even if accidental, including by the goalkeeper
- immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental
The goalkeeper has the same restrictions on handling the ball as any other player outside the penalty area. If the goalkeeper handles the ball inside their penalty area when not permitted to do so, an indirect free kick is awarded but there is no disciplinary sanction. However, if the offence is playing the ball a second time (with or without the hand/arm) after a restart before it touches another player, the goalkeeper must be sanctioned if the offence stops a promising attack or denies an opponent or the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity.

--- Law 12 | Denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity (DOGSO) [page 124, authoritative law text] ---
Where a player commits an offence against an opponent within their own penalty area which denies an opponent an obvious goal-scoring opportunity and the referee awards a penalty kick, the offender is cautioned if the offence was an attempt to play the ball or a challenge for the ball; in all other circumstances (e.g. holding, pulling, pushing, no possibility to play the ball etc.), the offending player must be sent off.
Where a player denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence, the player is sent off wherever the offence occurs (except a goalkeeper within their own penalty area).
Where a player denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence and the referee awards a penalty kick, the offender is cautioned.
A player, sent-off player, substitute or substituted player who enters the field of play without the required referee's permission and interferes with play or an opponent and denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity is guilty of a sending-off offence.
The following must be considered:
- distance between the offence and the goal
- general direction of the play
- likelihood of keeping or gaining control of the ball
- location and number of defenders and attackers

--- Law 12 | Sending-off offences [page 123, authoritative law text] ---
A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (except a goalkeeper within their penalty area)
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence outside their own penalty area
- denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity to an opponent whose overall movement is towards the offender's goal by an offence punishable by a free kick (unless as outlined below)
- serious foul play
- biting or spitting at someone
- violent conduct
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
A player, substitute or substituted player who has been sent off must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area.

title: Zidane headbutt — WC 2006 final
what_happened: In extra time, with the score at 1–1, Zinedine Zidane headbutted Marco Materazzi in the chest. The fourth official Luís Medina Cantalejo relayed the incident to referee Horacio Elizondo. Elizondo consulted his officials and showed Zidane a red card. Zidane walked past the World Cup trophy as he left the pitch. Italy won the subsequent penalty shoot-out 5–3.
explanation:
The decision to send off Zinedine Zidane with a red card was legally mandated under the IFAB Laws of the Game. According to **Law 12**, *Violent conduct* is defined as "when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent... regardless of whether contact is made." This clause directly applies because Zidane's headbutt against Materazzi constituted the use of excessive force against another player, meeting the criteria for violent conduct.

Furthermore, **Law 12** also outlines *Sending-off offences*, specifying that a player is sent off for "violent conduct." The text states, "A player... who commits any of the following offences is sent off:... violent conduct." Since Zidane's action clearly fell under the definition of violent conduct, the sending-off was required by the Laws.

Additionally, the law specifies that a player who is sent off "must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area." Zidane complied with this requirement by walking past the World Cup trophy as he exited, adhering to the procedural aspect of being sent off.

Thus, every component of the red card decision—identifying the act as violent conduct, applying the sending-off rule for such conduct, and ensuring the player's departure from the vicinity—was fully supported by the explicit provisions of the IFAB Laws of the Game. No discretion was involved; the laws dictated the outcome based on the described behavior.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Violent conduct [page 125, authoritative law text] ---
Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball, or against a team-mate, team official, match official, spectator or any other person, regardless of whether contact is made.
In addition, a player who, when not challenging for the ball, deliberately strikes an opponent or any other person on the head or face with the hand or arm, is guilty of violent conduct unless the force used was negligible.

--- Law 12 | Sending-off offences [page 123, authoritative law text] ---
A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (except a goalkeeper within their penalty area)
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence outside their own penalty area
- denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity to an opponent whose overall movement is towards the offender's goal by an offence punishable by a free kick (unless as outlined below)
- serious foul play
- biting or spitting at someone
- violent conduct
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
A player, substitute or substituted player who has been sent off must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area.

title: Henry handball in play-off — WC 2010 European qualifier
call: Goal given. No foul for handball awarded.
what_happened: With the aggregate score at 1–1 in the 103rd minute, a ball was played into the Irish penalty area. Thierry Henry controlled the ball with his left arm near the six-yard box, the ball touching his arm twice. Henry then passed to William Gallas who scored. Referee Martin Hansson did not call a foul. The goal stood. France advanced to the 2010 World Cup on an aggregate score of 2–1.
explanation:
In this incident, the key point is whether Thierry Henry's contact with the ball using his left arm constituted a handball offense under the Laws of the Game. According to Law 12, Section "It is an offence if a player," specifically clause "- deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, for example moving the hand/arm towards the ball," Henry's action appears to meet the criteria for a handball offense. The text states that a player commits an offense if they deliberately touch the ball with their hand or arm, which includes situations where the player moves their hand or arm towards the ball. In this case, Henry controlled the ball with his arm near the six-yard box, suggesting that he deliberately moved his arm towards the ball, thereby committing a handball offense.

Furthermore, Law 12 also states that a player is considered to have made their body unnaturally bigger if their hand or arm is not a consequence of or justifiable by their body movement for that specific situation. Clause "touches the ball with their hand/arm when it has made their body unnaturally bigger" implies that if Henry's arm position was not a natural consequence of his body movement, it would be considered an offense. Given that the ball touched his arm twice, it is likely that his arm position was not justified by his body movement, making it an unnatural extension of his body and thus an offense.

Under these clauses, the goal scored by William Gallas should have been disallowed because it was directly preceded by a handball offense committed by Thierry Henry. The Laws clearly state that a goal cannot be scored directly from a handball offense, nor can it be scored immediately after the ball has touched a player's hand or arm, even if accidental. Therefore, the correct decision would have been to award an indirect free kick to Ireland and disallow the goal.

However, the goal stood because the officials, including referee Martin Hansson, did not see or penalize the offense. The Laws emphasize that the decision to award or disallow a goal lies with the officials, and in this case, they failed to recognize the handball offense. As a result, the goal was allowed to stand, leading to France advancing to the 2010 World Cup on an aggregate score of 2-1. It is crucial to note that the goal standing was solely due to the officials' oversight and not because the Laws permitted it. The specific clauses quoted above demonstrate that the offense should have been penalized, and the goal should have been disallowed accordingly.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Handling the ball [page 116, authoritative law text] ---
For the purposes of determining handball offences, the upper boundary of the arm is in line with the bottom of the armpit. Not every touch of a player's hand/arm with the ball is an offence.

--- Law 12 | It is an offence if a player: [page 116, authoritative law text] ---
- deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, for example moving the hand/arm towards the ball
- touches the ball with their hand/arm when it has made their body unnaturally bigger. A player is considered to have made their body unnaturally bigger when the position of their hand/arm is not a consequence of, or justifiable by, the player's body movement for that specific situation. By having their hand/arm in such a position, the player takes a risk of their hand/arm being hit by the ball and being penalised
- scores in the opponents' goal:
- directly from their hand/arm, even if accidental, including by the goalkeeper
- immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental
The goalkeeper has the same restrictions on handling the ball as any other player outside the penalty area. If the goalkeeper handles the ball inside their penalty area when not permitted to do so, an indirect free kick is awarded but there is no disciplinary sanction. However, if the offence is playing the ball a second time (with or without the hand/arm) after a restart before it touches another player, the goalkeeper must be sanctioned if the offence stops a promising attack or denies an opponent or the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity.

title: Maradona handball goal — WC 1986 quarter-final
what_happened: In the 51st minute, Diego Maradona punched the ball into the goal inside the England penalty area with his left hand or fist. Referee Ali Bin Nasser awarded the goal. England players protested to the referee but the goal was not disallowed. Argentina won 2–1 and advanced to the semi-finals.
explanation:
In this incident, the Laws of the Game provide clear guidance on how the referee should have ruled. According to **Law 12 - Handling the ball**, specifically the clause stating "deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, for example moving the hand/arm towards the ball," Maradona's action of punching the ball into the goal constitutes a deliberate handball offense. This is further reinforced by the definition that "a player is considered to have made their body unnaturally bigger when the position of their hand/arm is not a consequence of, or justifiable by, the player's body movement for that specific situation." Maradona's punch clearly falls under this description, as it was a deliberate act to propel the ball into the goal.

Additionally, **Law 12** specifies that "if a player scores in the opponents' goal directly from their hand/arm, even if accidental, including by the goalkeeper, a goal must not be awarded." Since Maradona's handball directly led to the ball entering the goal, the correct decision would have been to disallow the goal. Furthermore, the law states that "immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental, a goal must not be awarded." Given that the ball entered the goal right after touching Maradona's hand, this clause also supports the need for the goal to be disallowed.

However, the goal stood because the referee, Ali Bin Nasser, did not see or penalize the offense. The laws make it clear that under these circumstances, the goal should not have been allowed. The decision to award the goal was an error by the officials, as they failed to recognize and enforce the applicable rules regarding handball offenses. Therefore, while the goal was incorrectly allowed to stand due to the officials' oversight, the proper application of the Laws of the Game dictates that the goal should have been disallowed.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Handling the ball [page 116, authoritative law text] ---
For the purposes of determining handball offences, the upper boundary of the arm is in line with the bottom of the armpit. Not every touch of a player's hand/arm with the ball is an offence.

--- Law 12 | It is an offence if a player: [page 116, authoritative law text] ---
- deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, for example moving the hand/arm towards the ball
- touches the ball with their hand/arm when it has made their body unnaturally bigger. A player is considered to have made their body unnaturally bigger when the position of their hand/arm is not a consequence of, or justifiable by, the player's body movement for that specific situation. By having their hand/arm in such a position, the player takes a risk of their hand/arm being hit by the ball and being penalised
- scores in the opponents' goal:
- directly from their hand/arm, even if accidental, including by the goalkeeper
- immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental
The goalkeeper has the same restrictions on handling the ball as any other player outside the penalty area. If the goalkeeper handles the ball inside their penalty area when not permitted to do so, an indirect free kick is awarded but there is no disciplinary sanction. However, if the offence is playing the ball a second time (with or without the hand/arm) after a restart before it touches another player, the goalkeeper must be sanctioned if the offence stops a promising attack or denies an opponent or the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity.

title: Tevez offside goal — WC 2010 round of 16
what_happened: In the 26th minute, Carlos Tevez headed the ball into the goal from inside the Mexican penalty area. Television replays broadcast on the stadium's big screens showed Tevez was in an offside position at the moment the ball was played to him. The assistant referee did not flag for offside. The goal stood. Argentina won 3–1. The match was played before VAR was introduced at the World Cup.
explanation:
The Laws of the Game provide clear guidance on how offside offences should be handled. In this incident, television replays confirmed that Carlos Tevez was in an offside position when the ball was played to him (Law 11 | 1). Specifically, any part of his head, body, or feet was nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent (Law 11 | 1).

According to the offside offence clause, a player in an offside position at the moment the ball is played or touched by a teammate is penalized if they become involved in active play by interfering with play or an opponent (Law 11 | 2). Tevez's involvement in scoring the goal constitutes interference with play, as he played the ball after it was touched by a teammate while in an offside position.

Furthermore, the offside offence clause states that a player in an offside position receiving the ball from an opponent who deliberately played the ball is not considered to have gained an advantage, unless it was a deliberate save by any opponent (Law 11 | 2). However, this exception does not apply here, as the ball was played by a teammate, not an opponent.

Given these clauses, the correct decision would have been to award an indirect free kick where the offside offence occurred (Law 11 | 4). The goal should have been disallowed, as Tevez's involvement in the attack from an offside position violated the Laws of the Game.

However, the goal stood because the assistant referee did not flag for the offside offence, and the match officials did not see or penalize the violation. The Laws make it clear that the goal should not have been allowed to stand, but due to the officials' error in not identifying and addressing the offside infraction, the incorrect decision was made to award the goal.
law_context_used:
--- Law 11 | 1. Offside position [page 109, authoritative law text] ---
It is not an offence to be in an offside position.
A player is in an offside position if:
- any part of the head, body or feet is in the opponents' half (excluding the halfway line) and
- any part of the head, body or feet is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent
The hands and arms of all players, including the goalkeepers, are not considered. For the purposes of determining offside, the upper boundary of the arm is in line with the bottom of the armpit.
A player is not in an offside position if level with the:
- second-last opponent or
- last two opponents

--- Law 11 | 2. Offside offence [page 109, authoritative law text] ---
A player in an offside position at the moment the ball is played or touched* by a team-mate is only penalised on becoming involved in active play by:
- interfering with play by playing or touching a ball passed or touched by a team-mate or
- interfering with an opponent by:
- preventing an opponent from playing or being able to play the ball by clearly obstructing the opponent's line of vision or
- challenging an opponent for the ball or
*The first point of contact of the 'play' or 'touch' of the ball should be used; however, when the ball is thrown by the goalkeeper, the last point of contact should be used.
or
- gaining an advantage by playing the ball or interfering with an opponent when it has:
- rebounded or been deflected off the goalpost, crossbar, match official or an opponent
- been deliberately saved by any opponent
A player in an offside position receiving the ball from an opponent who deliberately played* the ball, including by deliberate handball, is not considered to have gained an advantage, unless it was a deliberate save by any opponent.
*'Deliberate play' (excluding deliberate handball) is when a player has control of the ball with the possibility of:
- passing the ball to a team-mate;
- gaining possession of the ball; or
- clearing the ball (e.g. by kicking or heading it)
If the pass, attempt to gain possession or clearance by the player in control of the ball is inaccurate or unsuccessful, this does not negate the fact that the player 'deliberately played' the ball.
The following criteria should be used, as appropriate, as indicators that a player was in control of the ball and, as a result, can be considered to have 'deliberately played' the ball:

--- Law 11 | 4. Offences and sanctions [page 111, authoritative law text] ---
If an offside offence occurs, the referee awards an indirect free kick where the offence occurred, including if it is in the player's own half of the field of play.
A defending player who leaves the field of play without the referee's permission will be considered to be on the goal line or touchline for the purposes of offside until the next stoppage in play or until the defending team has played the ball towards the halfway line and it is outside its penalty area. If the player left the field of play deliberately, the player must be cautioned when the ball is next out of play.
An attacking player may step or stay off the field of play not to be involved in active play. If the player re-enters from the goal line and becomes involved in play before the next stoppage in play or the defending team has played the ball towards the halfway line and it is outside its penalty area, the player will be considered to be positioned on the goal line for the purposes of offside. A player who deliberately leaves the field of play and re-enters without the referee's permission and is not penalised for offside and gains an advantage must be cautioned.
If an attacking player remains stationary between the goalposts and inside the goal as the ball enters the goal, a goal must be awarded unless the player commits an offside offence or a Law 12 offence, in which case play is restarted with an indirect or direct free kick.

title: Keane serious foul play on Haaland — PL 2001
what_happened: Roy Keane tackled Alf-Inge Haaland with a late, forceful challenge to Haaland's knee. The referee showed Keane a red card. Haaland did not play professional football again due to the knee injuries sustained.
explanation:
The referee's decision to issue Roy Keane a red card for his tackle on Alf-Inge Haaland was legally correct according to the IFAB Laws of the Game.

Firstly, the tackle clearly constituted serious foul play, which mandates a sending-off. Law 12 states that "A tackle or challenge that endangers the safety of an opponent or uses excessive force or brutality must be sanctioned as serious foul play." Furthermore, it specifies that "Any player who lunges at an opponent in challenging for the ball from the front, from the side or from behind using one or both legs, with excessive force or endangers the safety of an opponent is guilty of serious foul play." Keane's late, forceful challenge directly endangered Haaland's safety by striking his knee, meeting the criteria for serious foul play.

Secondly, serious foul play is explicitly listed as a sending-off offense under Law 12. It states: "A player...is sent off if he commits serious foul play." Since Keane committed serious foul play against Haaland, the laws required the referee to send him off by showing a red card.

Additionally, the laws permit no exceptions for the seriousness of the foul. Even though it occurred late in the match, the laws make no distinction based on timing - any instance of serious foul play merits a red card. The consequences to Haaland's career are tragic but irrelevant to the legality of the call; the laws focus solely on the nature of the foul itself.

In summary, the referee correctly identified Keane's tackle as serious foul play endangering Haaland's safety, matched it to the corresponding sending-off offense in the laws, and issued the mandated red card. The laws left the referee no discretion to act otherwise given the facts of the incident.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Serious foul play [page 124, authoritative law text] ---
A tackle or challenge that endangers the safety of an opponent or uses excessive force or brutality must be sanctioned as serious foul play.
Any player who lunges at an opponent in challenging for the ball from the front, from the side or from behind using one or both legs, with excessive force or endangers the safety of an opponent is guilty of serious foul play.

--- Law 12 | Sending-off offences [page 123, authoritative law text] ---
A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (except a goalkeeper within their penalty area)
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence outside their own penalty area
- denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity to an opponent whose overall movement is towards the offender's goal by an offence punishable by a free kick (unless as outlined below)
- serious foul play
- biting or spitting at someone
- violent conduct
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
A player, substitute or substituted player who has been sent off must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area.

title: Suárez bites Chiellini — WC 2014 group stage
call: No action taken by the referee during the match.
what_happened: During the group-stage match, Luis Suárez bit Italian defender Giorgio Chiellini on the left shoulder. Chiellini pulled down his shirt to show the referee the bite mark. The referee took no action during the match. Uruguay won 1–0 and advanced from the group. FIFA retrospectively issued Suárez a nine-match international suspension and a four-month ban from all football-related activities.
explanation:
The incident involving Luis Suárez biting Giorgio Chiellini constitutes **violent conduct**, as defined in **Law 12** of the IFAB Laws of the Game. Specifically, the relevant clause states:

> "Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball, or against a team-mate, team official, match official, spectator or any other person, regardless of whether contact is made."

Additionally, the sending-off offences listed in **Law 12** include:

> "A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
> - ...
> - biting or spitting at someone
> - violent conduct
> ..."

Given that Suárez used excessive force by biting Chiellini, this action falls squarely under the definition of **violent conduct**. According to the laws, any player who commits violent conduct must be **sent off** immediately. Therefore, the correct sanction that should have been applied during the match was a **red card**, resulting in Suárez's ejection from the game.

However, the referee took **no action** during the match, failing to recognize or apply the appropriate sanction. This oversight means that the **correct sanction was not applied**. The failure to send off Suárez during the match was due to the officials not recognizing or enforcing the mandatory red card for violent conduct as stipulated in the laws.

It is important to note that this explanation pertains solely to the disciplinary action required under the laws and does not address any subsequent sanctions imposed by FIFA or the impact on the match result. The incorrect outcome was entirely due to the officials' failure to enforce the proper disciplinary measures as outlined in the laws.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Violent conduct [page 125, authoritative law text] ---
Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball, or against a team-mate, team official, match official, spectator or any other person, regardless of whether contact is made.
In addition, a player who, when not challenging for the ball, deliberately strikes an opponent or any other person on the head or face with the hand or arm, is guilty of violent conduct unless the force used was negligible.

--- Law 12 | Sending-off offences [page 123, authoritative law text] ---
A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (except a goalkeeper within their penalty area)
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence outside their own penalty area
- denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity to an opponent whose overall movement is towards the offender's goal by an offence punishable by a free kick (unless as outlined below)
- serious foul play
- biting or spitting at someone
- violent conduct
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
A player, substitute or substituted player who has been sent off must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area.

title: De Jong on Xabi Alonso — WC 2010 final
what_happened: Nigel de Jong jumped and planted the studs of his raised boot into Xabi Alonso's chest, striking him chest-high. The referee showed de Jong a yellow card. Spain won the match 1–0 after extra time.
explanation:
The incident involving Nigel de Jong lunging at Xabi Alonso with his raised boot clearly constitutes serious foul play, which mandates a sending-off according to the Laws of the Game. Specifically, Law 12 states:

"A tackle or challenge that endangers the safety of an opponent or uses excessive force or brutality must be sanctioned as serious foul play."

Furthermore, it specifies:

"Any player who lunges at an opponent in challenging for the ball from the front, from the side or from behind using one or both legs, with excessive force or endangers the safety of an opponent is guilty of serious foul play."

Given these clauses, de Jong's action of jumping and planting his studs into Alonso's chest, striking him chest-high, qualifies as serious foul play. According to Law 12 on sending-off offenses:

"A player...who commits...serious foul play is sent off."

Therefore, the correct sanction should have been a red card, resulting in de Jong's dismissal from the match. However, the referee incorrectly showed only a yellow card. This represents a clear error in officiating, as the Laws explicitly require a sending-off for such actions. The incorrect outcome was due to the officials failing to recognize or apply the appropriate sanction for serious foul play. Importantly, this explanation does not imply that any goal was disallowed or that the match result was affected, as the offense did not directly cause or prevent a goal.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Serious foul play [page 124, authoritative law text] ---
A tackle or challenge that endangers the safety of an opponent or uses excessive force or brutality must be sanctioned as serious foul play.
Any player who lunges at an opponent in challenging for the ball from the front, from the side or from behind using one or both legs, with excessive force or endangers the safety of an opponent is guilty of serious foul play.

--- Law 12 | Sending-off offences [page 123, authoritative law text] ---
A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (except a goalkeeper within their penalty area)
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence outside their own penalty area
- denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity to an opponent whose overall movement is towards the offender's goal by an offence punishable by a free kick (unless as outlined below)
- serious foul play
- biting or spitting at someone
- violent conduct
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
A player, substitute or substituted player who has been sent off must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area.

title: Rooney stamp — WC 2006 quarter-final
what_happened: Wayne Rooney stood on Ricardo Carvalho's groin with his foot during a challenge. The referee showed Rooney a straight red card. England played the rest of the match with ten men and lost on penalties.
explanation:
The referee's decision to issue a straight red card to Wayne Rooney was legally correct under the IFAB Laws of the Game.

Firstly, the incident clearly constituted "violent conduct" as defined in Law 12. The text states that violent conduct occurs when a player "uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent...regardless of whether contact is made." Quoting directly: "Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball...". Standing on an opponent's groin with one's foot during a tackle certainly qualifies as using excessive force and brutality, especially since Rooney was not actively challenging for the ball at that moment. This alone would have warranted a sending off.

Furthermore, Law 12 outlines specific sending-off offenses, one of which is "violent conduct". It states: "A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off: ...violent conduct..." Since Rooney committed violent conduct by standing on Carvalho's groin, he was required to be shown a red card per this clause.

Additionally, the laws specify that a player who is sent off "must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area." Therefore, showing Rooney a straight red card and ordering him to leave the pitch was mandatory once he committed the violent conduct offense.

In summary, the referee correctly identified Rooney's actions as violent conduct based on the explicit definition in Law 12, which then triggered the sending-off requirement for violent conduct offenses. The subsequent ejection from the vicinity of the pitch followed logically from the sending-off mandate. All components of the red card call were fully supported by and required under the specific clauses quoted from the authoritative IFAB law text.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Violent conduct [page 125, authoritative law text] ---
Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball, or against a team-mate, team official, match official, spectator or any other person, regardless of whether contact is made.
In addition, a player who, when not challenging for the ball, deliberately strikes an opponent or any other person on the head or face with the hand or arm, is guilty of violent conduct unless the force used was negligible.

--- Law 12 | Sending-off offences [page 123, authoritative law text] ---
A player, substitute or substituted player who commits any of the following offences is sent off:
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (except a goalkeeper within their penalty area)
- denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a non-deliberate handball offence outside their own penalty area
- denying a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity to an opponent whose overall movement is towards the offender's goal by an offence punishable by a free kick (unless as outlined below)
- serious foul play
- biting or spitting at someone
- violent conduct
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
A player, substitute or substituted player who has been sent off must leave the vicinity of the field of play and the technical area.

title: Di Canio pushes referee Alcock — PL 1998
call: Red card for Di Canio for pushing the referee.
what_happened: After being shown a red card, Paolo Di Canio pushed referee Paul Alcock, causing Alcock to fall to the ground. The referee showed Di Canio a red card for the push.
explanation:
The call made—issuing a red card to Paolo Di Canio for pushing the referee—was legally correct according to the IFAB Laws of the Game.

Firstly, the incident constitutes **violent conduct** as defined in Law 12. Specifically, Clause 1 states that "violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent...or against...any other person." By pushing the referee, Di Canio used force against a match official, which falls squarely under this definition. The clause does not require contact with the ball; it applies broadly to actions against others, including officials.

Secondly, Law 12 also outlines the sanctions for such behavior. Clause 2 specifies that sending-off offenses include "violent conduct." Since Di Canio's push qualifies as violent conduct, the appropriate sanction is a red card, which results in his immediate dismissal from the match. This aligns with the sending-off provisions detailed in Law 12, where Clause 1 lists "violent conduct" among the behaviors warranting a red card.

Additionally, the referee's decision to show Di Canio a red card was consistent with the authority granted under these laws. The laws empower referees to issue red cards for violent conduct, ensuring they can maintain control and safety during matches. By issuing the red card, the referee acted within their jurisdiction to enforce the rules and protect all participants, including himself.

In summary, the red card issued to Di Canio for pushing the referee was mandated by the explicit provisions of Law 12 regarding violent conduct and the corresponding sending-off offense. The referee correctly applied these laws to address the misconduct, upholding the integrity and safety of the game.
law_context_used:
--- Law 12 | Violent conduct [page 125, authoritative law text] ---
Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball, or against a team-mate, team official, match official, spectator or any other person, regardless of whether contact is made.
In addition, a player who, when not challenging for the ball, deliberately strikes an opponent or any other person on the head or face with the hand or arm, is guilty of violent conduct unless the force used was negligible.

--- Law 12 | Sending-off [page 126, authoritative law text] ---
Sending-off offences include (but are not limited to):
- delaying the restart of play by the opposing team e.g. holding onto the ball, kicking the ball away, obstructing the movement of a player
- deliberately leaving the technical area to:
- show dissent towards, or remonstrate with, a match official
- act in a provocative or inflammatory manner
- entering the opposing technical area in an aggressive or confrontational manner
- deliberately throwing/kicking an object onto the field of play
- entering the field of play to:
- confront a match official (including at half-time and full-time)
- interfere with play, an opposing player or a match official
- entering the video operation room (VOR)
- physical or aggressive behaviour (including spitting or biting) towards an opposing player, substitute, team official, match official, spectator or any other person (e.g. ball boy/girl, security or competition official etc.)
- receiving a second caution in the same match
- using offensive, insulting or abusive language and/or action(s)
- using unauthorised electronic or communication equipment and/or behaving in an inappropriate manner as a result of using electronic or communication equipment
- violent conduct
